note: Each archetype includes: a long roasty description (longDesc), a result-page quote (quote), an SVG portrait description (svgPortrait), and a portraitPrompt for image generation.

entries.name: The Waterboy
entries.shortDesc: You mostly experience basketball through friends, memes, and unfortunate proximity.
entries.quote: Wait, tip-off already? I thought we were just hanging out.
entries.longDesc:
Basketball mostly happens around you, not inside you. You know enough to nod at the right moments and maybe ask a safe question, but not enough for your pulse to change when somebody starts yelling about legacy, officiating, or lottery odds.

The funny part is that you still made it all the way through this quiz. That means somebody dragged you here, or you are curious enough to peek into a culture you do not fully live in. Either way, you earned the last seat on the bench: towel in hand, zero pressure, immaculate peace.
entries.svgPortrait: A person in an obviously too-big jersey sits at the very end of a bench, both hands clutching a bubble tea and a phone, staring off to the side completely checked out. Behind them, a wall of silhouetted fans goes wild. A few unopened programs are on the floor. Mood: out of place, unbothered, random passerby. Palette: cool grays and blues, with basketball orange only on the background fans for contrast.
entries.portraitPrompt: Humorous cartoon-style basketball bystander, oversized jersey in the corner of a bench, bubble tea and phone in hand, clearly zoned out, roaring fans behind them, orange-black arena backdrop, cool gray-blue tones emphasizing the disconnect.

entries.name: The Six-Pack Spectator
entries.shortDesc: For you, the game is half basketball and half drinking event with a scoreboard attached.
entries.quote: Do not tip it yet. I need to open one more.
entries.longDesc:
You treat basketball like a social function that just happens to involve rebounds and defensive breakdowns. Tip-off is the signal to crack something open, the second quarter is when your volume gets irresponsible, and by the fourth you are fully arguing with the television like it insulted your family.

You are not fake. If anything, you care too much. You just process that care through beer, snacks, yelling, and extremely poor emotional regulation. The next morning the box score is fuzzy, but the emotional damage is always crystal clear.
entries.svgPortrait: A person in an unzipped jersey stands in a cluttered living room in front of the TV, one hand raising a beer bottle, the other jabbing at the screen mid-yell. The coffee table is covered in empty cans, snacks, and a phone. The TV shows a blurry live game. His face is flushed, mouth wide open. A kicked-over slipper on the floor. A crooked team flag tacked to the wall. Palette: warm yellow light, basketball orange, beer gold.
entries.portraitPrompt: Exaggerated cartoon-style drunk basketball fan, unzipped team jersey, standing in living room in front of TV, beer raised, yelling, flushed face, crushed cans and snacks on table, messy cheer gear, warm amber lighting.

entries.name: The GOAT Arbiter
entries.shortDesc: Every basketball conversation within range eventually becomes your latest all-time ranking.
entries.quote: Give me ten seconds. I need to rework the top ten first.
entries.longDesc:
Nobody gets to say something nice about a player around you without accidentally reopening the all-time list. One compliment becomes peak-versus-longevity, then rings, then supporting cast, then suddenly the room is trapped inside your tenth revision of the top ten.

The important part is that this is not trolling. You genuinely enjoy the sorting. Ranking players is how you clean the kitchen of your own basketball brain. Everybody else finds it exhausting. You find it deeply satisfying.
entries.svgPortrait: A stern person stands in front of a massive whiteboard crowded with player names and ranking numbers, some crossed out, some with arrows repositioning them. Red marker in hand, revising live. Binders and printed stat sheets stacked on a table. GOAT posters on the wall, each with their own ranking sticker. Palette: orange accent lighting, dark background, bright whiteboard as focal point.
entries.portraitPrompt: Comic poster-style sports pundit fan, standing in a room covered in GOAT posters, holding binders and a red marker, whiteboard of rankings behind, intense and authoritative expression, warm orange lighting, printed stat sheets scattered.

entries.name: The Brick Prophet
entries.shortDesc: You talk with prophet confidence and jinx-with-receipts timing.
entries.quote: Lock it in. Actually hold on, let me delete that.
entries.longDesc:
You call games early, loudly, and with complete emotional commitment. "We are winning by 20." "Series over." "Book the parade." Then the game flips, your old messages get screenshotted, and now you are the evening's entertainment.

The wild thing is that you never really stop. Being publicly wrong is just the cover charge for having takes. You would rather be loudly embarrassed than quietly absent, and while that makes you hilarious, it also makes you weirdly brave.
entries.svgPortrait: A person stands in front of a giant scoreboard showing a brutal loss for their team, one hand holding up a screenshot of a bold pre-game prediction ("MY TEAM BY 30!"), the other covering their face. Behind them, group chat screenshots project "LMAO" reactions. Their jersey number reads "0-27." Expression: stubborn embarrassment. Palette: red-orange dominant with awkward pink overlays.
entries.portraitPrompt: Exaggerated comedic fan, in jersey labeled 0-27, standing in front of scoreboard showing loss, holding up bold pre-game prediction screenshot, face partially covered, group chat roast reactions floating behind, stubborn embarrassed expression.

entries.name: The Process Truther
entries.shortDesc: A loss feels bad for other people. For you, it can feel productive.
entries.quote: Do not win tonight. Think long-term.
entries.longDesc:
A normal fan sees a loss and thinks, this stinks. You see a loss and start doing draft-position math. One useless March win can annoy you for days. A ten-game skid can feel like the franchise finally making responsible choices.

This is not passive suffering. It is organized hope. You are already watching grainy clips of teenagers with seven-foot wingspans and convincing yourself they can fix everything by 2029. The season may be cooked, but the future still has heat.
entries.svgPortrait: A person kneels in front of a giant "LOTTERY" ball machine, hands pressed together in prayer, expression devout. Their old jersey is covered in printed photos of draft prospects. A "Trust The Process" banner hangs above. Table covered in scouting reports and draft board comps. Crossed-out season records litter the floor. Palette: blue-gray base, gold lottery balls, hint of hope-glow.
entries.portraitPrompt: Humorous comic-style rebuilding fan, old jersey, kneeling at a lottery ball machine in prayer, screen behind showing draft prospects and stats, Trust The Process banner overhead, fanatical devout expression.

entries.name: The Armchair Coach
entries.shortDesc: You have fired a thousand coaches from the couch and still think you could do it better.
entries.quote: Put me in. Not at point guard. On the sideline.
entries.longDesc:
Timeouts belong to you in spirit. Rotations belong to you in principle. Every late-game possession becomes a fresh opportunity to point at the screen and explain exactly what should have happened two passes ago.

The problem is that you are not completely clueless, which is what makes you dangerous. You know enough terminology and spacing logic to sound real. Unfortunately, drawing the action and getting five live humans to execute it are two entirely different professions, and you only practice one of them.
entries.svgPortrait: A person in a hoodie stands in front of their living room TV holding a whiteboard and a marker, drawing plays as the broadcast shows a paused timeout. Hand-drawn plays on napkins scatter the coffee table, a whistle and a remote in frame. Deadly serious expression, directing invisible players. An indifferent cat sits on the couch. Palette: dark blue-gray background, whiteboard white, basketball orange arrows.
entries.portraitPrompt: Comic-style armchair coach fan, hoodie, clutching whiteboard and marker in front of TV, paused-timeout broadcast, scribbled play diagrams, whistle on couch, intense expression directing invisible players, unbothered cat nearby.

entries.name: The Analytics Enforcer
entries.shortDesc: You do not trust vibes when a spreadsheet exists.
entries.quote: Do not tell me how it felt. Show me the numbers.
entries.longDesc:
While everybody else is talking about killer instinct, confidence, and "you just had to watch it," you are already opening tabs. TS%, on/off, matchup data, shot profile, opponent quality: if the conversation cannot survive contact with numbers, you do not think much of the conversation.

To your friends, talking hoops with you can feel like being audited. To you, that is exactly the point. Basketball gets cleaner when the data is on the table. If someone's argument falls apart the second the stats arrive, maybe it deserved to.
entries.svgPortrait: A person in glasses sits at a desk with three wraparound monitors, each covered in basketball dashboards, line charts, and Excel grids. One hand holds coffee, the other points at a data spike — calm face with a hint of superiority. Shelves of stats books and printouts around them. Room is dark, only screens lighting the face. Palette: tech blue, data green, deep dark background.
entries.portraitPrompt: Tech-forward comic-style analytics fan, glasses, seated at multi-monitor setup, screens showing advanced basketball stats and spreadsheets, coffee in hand, cold confident expression, dark home office lit only by screens.

entries.name: Mr. 58-Point Benchmark
entries.shortDesc: Every ugly score has to pass through your private 58-point conversion table.
entries.quote: Only lost by 50? That is still just 0.86 of a 58.
entries.longDesc:
In your head, 58 is not just a score anymore. It is a measuring device.

On April 27, 2009, the Nuggets beat the Hornets 121-63 in New Orleans and handed Chris Paul the full 58-point humiliation at home. Your brain never moved on. Every blowout since then gets dragged back to that box score like it is the official unit of basketball embarrassment.

That means you cannot just say a team got smoked. You have to contextualize it. Was it truly catastrophic, or merely 0.86-of-a-58 catastrophic? People find this unbearable. They are right. They are also wrong if they think you are going to stop preserving the lore.
entries.svgPortrait: A person stands beside a massive ruler where the largest mark is 58, starred and highlighted; smaller marks say 20, 40, 50. He holds a pointer at the 58 line, smug and focused. Behind him, a collage of blowout scoreboards. Shirt reads "0.86 of a 58." Palette: black base, gold tick marks, red score numbers.
entries.portraitPrompt: Ironic comic-style meme fan, holding a giant ruler with 58 starred, standing in front of a blowout scoreboard collage, smug expression, meme shirt, black background with gold ticks and red scores.

entries.name: The Bandwagon Ace
entries.shortDesc: Your fandom history looks suspiciously similar to a title timeline.
entries.quote: I am not chasing rings. I am appreciating excellence.
entries.longDesc:
Your team has a funny habit of being whichever team currently looks smartest, deepest, or most likely to be covered in confetti two months from now. New wallpaper, new jersey, new explanation for why this contender just happens to fit your values perfectly.

You hate the term bandwagon fan because you think it sounds too cheap. In your version of events, you are simply drawn to good basketball. Conveniently, good basketball keeps ending with trophies. That is not hypocrisy to you. That is taste.
entries.svgPortrait: A person layers five or six different team jerseys, each peeking out at the edges. Arms full of this year's champion merch — cap, scarf, flag. Old jerseys lie discarded on the floor. Confident, unbothered expression ("this is normal"). Multiple franchise championship silhouettes overlap in the background. Palette: rainbow collage, gold championship glow.
entries.portraitPrompt: Over-the-top cartoon-style bandwagon fan, layered multiple team jerseys, arms full of latest champion merch, old team caps tossed on the floor, unfazed smile, multiple title celebrations overlapping behind.

entries.name: The Kiss of Death
entries.shortDesc: The moment you declare something safe, the universe starts scripting disaster.
entries.quote: I did not jinx it. I just happened to be there when it broke.
entries.longDesc:
You are not trying to doom your team. That would almost be easier to explain. The problem is that every time you tweet "we are good" or post something prematurely confident, reality immediately starts looking for a way to embarrass you.

The people around you have noticed. No early victory posts. No locked-in memes. No celebratory screenshots before the buzzer. Because with you, optimism does not feel hopeful. It feels like a risk factor.
entries.svgPortrait: A person frozen in front of the TV, phone screen showing a just-sent "LOCKED IN" message. The broadcast score flips in real time — their team's points plummet. Expression: collapsed innocence. Ghost of the J.R. Smith frozen-face moment floats behind him. Group chat pings: "It's you again." Palette: melancholic gray-blue, red alert score, green chat bubbles.
entries.portraitPrompt: Humorous comic-style jinx fan, frozen mid-send of 'locked in' text as TV score reverses, devastated innocent face, JRSmith ghost silhouette behind, group chat 'shut up' pings floating, gray-blue with red alert.

entries.name: The Hater in Chief
entries.shortDesc: Your emotional well-being is heavily indexed to one specific rival failing.
entries.quote: Sure, they won. Still a fraudulent path though.
entries.longDesc:
You do not always need your own team to win in order to feel joy. Sometimes it is enough for the team you hate to lose. Sometimes, if we are being honest, it is even better.

What makes you dangerous is that your hatred is organized. You remember lucky whistles, soft schedules, media overpraise, fake underdog stories, all of it. Other fans dislike rivals. You maintain archives.
entries.svgPortrait: A person arms crossed in front of a TV broadcasting a rival's championship parade. Exaggerated scowl. Thought bubbles float around him: "weak schedule," "soft opponents," "asterisk ring." A notepad beside him is scribbled with negative stat lines about their star. Palette: sour yellow-green, cold gray base, glinting championship gold on-screen for contrast.
entries.portraitPrompt: Exaggerated comic-style salty fan, arms crossed scowling at TV championship parade, floating lemons and hot-take bubbles, notepad full of anti-star stats, sour yellow-green against gold trophy glow.

entries.name: The Ref Truther
entries.shortDesc: A bad whistle is never random to you. It is evidence.
entries.quote: Wait, run it back at quarter speed. There it is.
entries.longDesc:
To you, a missed call is never just one bad call. It belongs in a chain. There is always a previous example, a suspicious pattern, a too-convenient outcome, and a replay angle that mysteriously makes everything look worse.

That does not mean you think every game is fixed. It means you are never fully convinced any big moment is innocent. Slow motion is your best friend. Freeze-frames are your language. A whistle swallowed at the wrong time can keep you busy for days.
entries.svgPortrait: A person clutching a remote stands in front of a TV frozen on a slow-motion controversial call, a red circle drawn over a foot on a line. Other hand holds a magnifying glass. Around him float whistles, red cards, and "RIGGED!" bubbles. Table scattered with printed LTMR pages. Palette: deep red indignation, black background, TV blue light.
entries.portraitPrompt: Comic-style outraged fan clutching remote and magnifier at TV with slow-mo controversial call, red circles on screen, whistles and 'rigged' bubbles floating, LTMR printouts on table.

entries.name: The Fire-Everyone Guy
entries.shortDesc: When things go bad, your first instinct is to start firing people in batches.
entries.quote: Three straight losses and everybody still has a job?
entries.longDesc:
For you, every basketball problem belongs to the same solution family: fire the coach, trade the soft guys, dump the front office, sell the team, start over. Nuance slows the process down. You prefer names, blame, and immediate action.

This is why group chats come alive when you enter after a losing streak. You bring clarity. Reckless, overclocked, borderline ungovernable clarity, but clarity all the same. Other people want context. You want exits.
entries.svgPortrait: A person on an arena concourse holds up two banners: left reads "COACH OUT," right reads "GM OUT." Mouth wide open mid-chant. Fans nearby are either taking photos or edging away. Background: losing streak headlines and a coach walking out of the tunnel with his head down. Crumpled trade-machine printouts at his feet. Palette: angry red, black background, sharp white text.
entries.portraitPrompt: Poster-style angry fan in arena concourse holding 'FIRE HIM' banners, yelling, fans photographing and avoiding him, background shows losing streak headlines, crumpled trade proposals underfoot, red dominant.

entries.name: The Highlight Disciple
entries.shortDesc: If nobody got put on a poster, part of you thinks the game was spiritually incomplete.
entries.quote: That was nice, but did anybody get baptized?
entries.longDesc:
You can respect shooting and spacing in theory, but what you really came for is violence at the rim. One clean poster does more for your soul than six possessions of beautiful half-court execution.

That is because basketball, to you, is supposed to feel physical and immediate. You want noise, bodies, momentum, and at least one moment where the whole room stands up at once. The box score can explain the rest later.
entries.svgPortrait: A person tilts his head up at a giant silhouette mid-dunk in the sky — eyes blazing, mouth open, fists clenched. Phone tilted horizontally recording. Around him, the rest of a normal basketball play — pass, screen, off-ball run — is greyed out and blurred. Only the dunk frame is in full color. Palette: hot red-orange, high contrast, grayed-out tactical elements.
entries.portraitPrompt: High-energy comic-style dunk fan, head back at a giant silhouette dunking overhead, eyes glowing, filming on phone, rest of play grayed out, only dunk frame in full color, fiery red-orange palette.

entries.name: The Logo-Shot Evangelist
entries.shortDesc: Deep threes are not all equal in your mind. Steph has his own category.
entries.quote: When he takes it from there, that is not a bad shot. That is doctrine.
entries.longDesc:
You do not just like three-point shooting. You believe in it, and more specifically, you believe in a very particular, very holy form of it that begins with Steph and, in your opinion, mostly ends there too.

That is why your standards are so warped. Curry from 32 feet is art. Somebody else from 32 feet might need a review. You are not really evaluating distance or difficulty. You are evaluating whether the shooter has earned access to your religion.
entries.svgPortrait: A person arms raised in worship, staring up at a basketball tracing an impossibly high arc toward a distant hoop. Logo at mid-court marked under their feet. Huge arena in the background with dramatic lights. Expression: devout awe. Clothed in number-30 color scheme. Palette: gold/blue (Warriors hint), orange ball trail, halo glow.
entries.portraitPrompt: Comic-style three-point evangelist, arms raised in worship, looking up at high-arc logo shot, logo marking on floor, massive arena stadium lighting, fervent devout expression, gold-blue with orange ball trail halo.

entries.name: The Buzzer-Beater Believer
entries.shortDesc: You never fully believe a game is over because miracles have already rewired your brain.
entries.quote: Do not turn it off. Weird things happen fast.
entries.longDesc:
Down twelve late is not a death sentence to you. It is an invitation to remember every insane comeback clip that ever lodged itself in your brain and convinced you that no broadcast is truly safe to leave early.

There is something kind of beautiful about that. Irrational, yes, but beautiful. Basketball is a sport of runs, hot hands, and absurd endings, and you are always emotionally ready for one more miracle even when everybody else has accepted the final score already.
entries.svgPortrait: A person, fists clenched, stands in front of a TV showing 0.5 seconds left and a brutal deficit. Expression is not despair — it's tight, hopeful anticipation, eyes bright. Behind him drift translucent ghost frames of T-Mac's 13-in-35, Reggie's 8-in-8.9, Dame's 0.9. Morning light already outside the window; he hasn't moved. Palette: dramatic warm orange, red score tension, glow.
entries.portraitPrompt: Romantic comic-style comeback believer, fists clenched facing TV with 0.5 seconds and deficit, hopeful intense expression, translucent legendary game-winner frames floating behind, dawn outside, dramatic warm orange lighting.

entries.name: The Ride-or-Die Martyr
entries.shortDesc: If the team goes down, you go down with them. That is the contract.
entries.quote: I am not leaving. Somebody has to stay and suffer correctly.
entries.longDesc:
You are not switching teams. Not after the bad years, not during the bad years, not if the bad years somehow become their own geological period. You can complain about the roster, drag the coach, roast ownership, and still refuse to walk away.

That is because your loyalty is not gentle. It is bruised, stubborn, occasionally irrational, and completely permanent. Outsiders call it unhealthy. You call it being real.
entries.svgPortrait: A person in a washed-out faded jersey stands alone outside a decaying arena. Letters missing from the arena sign. Holding a team scarf in both hands, expression tired but set. Behind him, a wall plastered with years of season ticket stubs and losing headlines — but the top one reads "this year." Palette: warm faded sepia, muted team colors, steady orange eye-light.
entries.portraitPrompt: Weighty comic-style diehard, faded team jersey standing outside worn-down arena holding scarf, behind him wall of ticket stubs and losing-season clippings with top one reading 'this year,' tired but resolute face, sepia tones.

entries.name: The Lone Wolf Spectator
entries.shortDesc: You like your basketball quiet, private, and free of extra commentary.
entries.quote: I do not need company to know what I just watched.
entries.longDesc:
You do not hate people. You just think most of them make watching basketball worse. Watch parties talk through possessions. Group chats flatten moments before you can think about them. Somebody always needs something explained.

So you watch alone. Not because you care less, but because you care in a cleaner way. You would rather process the game in full than perform fandom for an audience.
entries.svgPortrait: A person sitting alone in a dark living room late at night, lit only by the blue-white of the TV and a faint phone glow. Leaned back on the couch. Expression calm and cool — neither hyped nor sad. A glass of water and an open Notes app on the coffee table. A 4am cityscape outside the window. Nobody else. Palette: deep navy, cold screen white, faint warm city lights.
entries.portraitPrompt: Cool-toned comic-style lone fan, alone late-night living room watching game, only TV and phone light, calm detached face, water glass and notes app on table, 4am skyline outside, undisturbed solitude.

entries.name: The Stat-Padding Auditor
entries.shortDesc: Every huge scoring night gets an immediate asterisk check from you.
entries.quote: Forty is cute. Open the garbage-time filter.
entries.longDesc:
Big counting stats do not move you on their own. Somebody drops 40 and the room starts celebrating. You are already checking pace, usage, shot diet, opponent quality, and how much of that damage happened after the game had already gone soft.

That does not make you anti-stats. It makes you anti-lazy stats. If the performance is real, you will respect it. If it smells inflated, you are bringing out the asterisk before the highlights finish rendering.
entries.svgPortrait: A person holding a giant magnifying glass, inspecting a scoreboard packed with player stats. Expression: stern, picky — a quality inspector scanning for defects. Around him float red labels reading "padded," "empty," "fake stats." Behind him two comparison boxes labeled "him" (X'd out) and "my guy" (checkmark), with identical numbers. Palette: audit red, warning yellow, cold gray background.
entries.portraitPrompt: Satirical comic-style stat auditor, giant magnifier over scoreboard, red 'padded' and 'empty' labels floating, two side-by-side comparison boxes with identical stats but opposite verdicts, audit red and warning yellow palette.

entries.name: The 'Called It' Oracle
entries.shortDesc: You love being right early almost as much as you love the game itself.
entries.quote: Scroll up. I said this was coming.
entries.longDesc:
Predictions are not little throwaway comments to you. They are investments. If one hits, you are not moving on until everyone in the chat has acknowledged that you called it before the rest of the herd caught on.

What makes this dangerous is that you are not just loud. You are right often enough to build a reputation around it. That is how a few good calls turn into a full oracle complex.
entries.svgPortrait: A person sitting in front of their phone, which shows a pre-game prediction message validated by the final score, big green check next to it. Mysterious smug half-smile, cheek propped on one hand. Behind them: a crystal-ball-shaped basketball and several bracket grids. Table covered in prediction scribbles. Palette: mysterious purple, oracle gold, phone-screen white.
entries.portraitPrompt: Comic-style oracle fan, seated at phone showing validated prediction, smug mystery smile, crystal-ball basketball and bracket grid backdrop, prediction notes scattered, purple and gold.

entries.name: The Finals-Only Fan
entries.shortDesc: You reappear when the lights get bright and disappear when the schedule gets ordinary.
entries.quote: Wait, is number 23 LeBron or Jordan in this context?
entries.longDesc:
You are not around all year, and you are not pretending otherwise. Finals season, Christmas, big trades, legacy arguments, maybe one or two marquee games if the timeline gets loud enough. The rest of the calendar barely knows you.

Weirdly, that honesty makes you less annoying than the people faking diehard status off three nationally televised games. You wander in with missing details, loud confidence, and extremely seasonal devotion. It is fake, yes, but refreshingly unpretentious fake.
entries.svgPortrait: A person in a still-creased brand-new champion tee, fumbling for a phone photo of the TV. Phone search bar visible: "STARTING 5 2026 [TEAM]." Around him, brand new team merch with tags still on. TV shows Finals footage. Expression: trying to fake knowing, eyes betraying confusion. Palette: fresh-out-the-box white, TV blue glow, embarrassed pink flush.
entries.portraitPrompt: Comedic comic-style fake fan, crease-new champion tee photographing TV, search bar open to roster question, tagged merch nearby, TV shows Finals, bluffing expression, fresh white and TV blue with nervous pink.

entries.name: The Vibes Captain
entries.shortDesc: The basketball matters, but the chat reaction, screenshots, and energy matter almost as much.
entries.quote: Huge shot. Bigger group-chat moment.
entries.longDesc:
For you, the game is only half the event. The other half is the all-caps reaction, the first screenshot, the perfectly timed post, the little social explosion that proves everybody else felt it too.

That does not mean you do not care about basketball. It means you care about basketball as a live shared experience. A dagger is great. A dagger that detonates the chat is better. Without the audience, some of the electricity leaks out.
entries.svgPortrait: A person in eye-popping jersey stands in the middle of a crowd, one hand livestreaming on a phone, the other hoisting a handmade sign. Around them: chat text, exclamation points, emojis, cheer effects. Mouth wide open mid-scream. Background is a blurred energetic crowd. Palette: high-saturation rainbow, energy orange, party vibes.
entries.portraitPrompt: Energetic comic-style hype fan, loud jersey in center of crowd, streaming on phone and waving sign, surrounded by chat bubbles exclamation emojis cheer effects, mouth wide open shouting, saturated rainbow plus energy orange.

entries.name: The Zen Spectator
entries.shortDesc: You care about basketball without letting it hijack your nervous system.
entries.quote: If I catch it, great. If not, life continues.
entries.longDesc:
Bad loss? Tough. Great win? Nice. Wild finish? Interesting. You are engaged, but never fully captured, which makes you look alien inside fan spaces built on overreaction.

That is not detachment. It is discipline. You found a way to keep sports in the sports bucket, and while that might make you less dramatic than the rest of us, it also means you do not spend your nights being emotionally waterboarded by final possessions.
entries.svgPortrait: A person cross-legged on the couch, a cup of tea nearby. TV shows a walk-off buzzer-beater — scoreboard flipping, crowd erupting — but his expression is serene, even slightly sleepy. Phone beside him is dark. Moon visible through the window. He wears a soft, very old team tee. Palette: minimal gray-blue, warm tea tones, loud TV moment contrasting quietly.
entries.portraitPrompt: Minimalist comic-style zen fan, cross-legged on couch watching serenely, buzzer-beater on TV, tea beside him, dark phone, old team tee, moonlight outside, gray-blue palette against the loud TV moment.

entries.name: The League Pass ATM
entries.shortDesc: Your fandom absolutely has a transaction history.
entries.quote: This is not overspending. This is supporting the cause.
entries.longDesc:
League Pass, jerseys, sneakers, tickets, random collabs, collector nonsense you definitely did not need but bought anyway — your love of basketball leaves a paper trail.

The key thing is that you almost never experience this as reckless. There is always a story attached: rare drop, special season, once-in-a-lifetime seat, meaningful colorway. That is how devotion talks when it has access to a debit card and no adult supervision.
entries.svgPortrait: A person standing in front of a wall of framed jerseys, a sneaker display case, and rookie card cabinets — essentially a private museum. Holding a game ticket and a credit card, proud. Several unopened shoe boxes and shipping boxes at his feet. Palette: gold display lighting, dark wood cases, basketball orange highlights.
entries.portraitPrompt: Glam comic-style collector fan, in front of framed jerseys, sneaker cases, rookie card cabinets, holding ticket and credit card, proud, unopened shoe boxes at feet, gold lighting and dark wood.

entries.name: The Pickup Legend (Self-Declared)
entries.shortDesc: Your confidence arrives at the court well before your actual jumper does.
entries.quote: I can make that. The conditions are just weird today.
entries.longDesc:
You believe in your game with a level of commitment the evidence does not always reward. Misses are contextual. Rim was off. Legs were dead. Sun was weird. The shot itself was fine.

That is why pickup is such a perfect habitat for you. It gives you endless room to imagine the better version of yourself and just enough real action to keep the fantasy alive. Every brick has an excuse. Every make is prophecy fulfilled.
entries.svgPortrait: A person on a blacktop park mid-follow-through of an exaggerated step-back fadeaway, off one foot, radiating confidence. The ball's arc is obviously off, heading the wrong way, miles from the rim. A friend behind them is cracking up with their head in their hands. Passersby film it. He's wearing full compression sleeves, knee braces, branded socks — gear stacked. Palette: concrete gray, sky blue, comedic warm yellow.
entries.portraitPrompt: Exaggerated comic-style pickup-legend, exaggerated step-back fade missing badly, friend laughing behind, passersby filming, overgeared in sleeves and braces, park court, concrete gray and sky blue, comedic warm yellow light.

entries.name: The Team Mom
entries.shortDesc: You do not just root for players. You worry about them like they are your own overworked kids.
entries.quote: Please sit him for a week. I am asking nicely.
entries.longDesc:
Minutes load, injury history, recovery timelines, coaching decisions, media pressure — all of it lands in your brain as a form of concern. You can enjoy the game, sure, but your first instinct is still protective.

That makes you a very specific kind of fan. Somebody around here has to remember these are human beings, not just pieces in a title window. Win later. Ice the ankle now.
entries.svgPortrait: A person wrapped in a team scarf, holding up a team sign in one hand, the other hand gently pressed to their chest. Expression: soft worry — not for the standings, for his knees. TV shows a player hitting the floor in slow motion. A mug of hot tea and a symbolic bandaid on the coffee table. Palette: warm beige, soft anxious blue, team color accents.
entries.portraitPrompt: Tender comic-style worried fan, wrapped in team scarf hand on chest watching slow-mo player fall on TV, hot tea and symbolic bandaid on table, parental concern expression, warm beige and soft blue.

entries.name: Basketball Switzerland
entries.shortDesc: You are weirdly okay with almost everything, which makes you suspiciously healthy.
entries.quote: Whoever. Whatever. I can watch that.
entries.longDesc:
Strong hatred does not really live in you. Favorite team, sure. Favorite player, fine. But the idea of letting sports colonize your entire emotional system never fully took.

In some fan circles that makes you look unserious. In healthier circles it makes you look evolved. You are proof that a person can like basketball without turning every outcome into a referendum on their soul.
entries.svgPortrait: A person in a plain jacket with no team logo sitting in the exact middle of a couch. To each side, scarves and merch from two different teams, neither picked up. Expression calm and untroubled, remote in hand, ready to change the channel at any moment. TV plays a game, but he could just as easily be watching anything else. Palette: neutral gray, scattered team colors that never dominate, soft natural light.
entries.portraitPrompt: Neutral comic-style laid-back fan, plain no-logo jacket seated center couch, both team scarves flanking but untouched, remote in hand calm expression, neutral gray palette with scattered team colors that don't dominate.
